body_txt: Describe Against My Religion Here, unless describing tropes is forbidden by your creed. Tolerance of one another's religious beliefs is a common mindset today in many cultures ("today" meaning many, but not all, countries in the 20 and 21st centuries. It was in place earlier, but more narrowly). In works created by people from those cultures, if someone says something is against their religion, other characters think they can't make them do it. Now that person might be telling Blatant Lies to get out of doing that thing, but it might be the truth. Other characters are likely not going to press that character about it. If the character is trying to make a point in The War on Straw , or simply trying to abuse their religious status to avoid some unpleasant obligation , they may invoke (or create) a Parody Religion that just so happens to be against the thing they don't want to do. Freedom of religion does entail that you are legally allowed to hold any creed you choose to believe so Loophole Abuse may ensue. Comes up a lot with someone raised Catholic, Good Girls Avoid Abortion in particular. Of course, in Real Life , many practices considered common by the majority may be expressly forbidden to people of a particular religious group. A common loophole use of this in real-life is that some people believe that they have a guardian angel/conscience/etc. that enables them to judge things as wrong which are not explicitly banned by their religion, and then requires that they do what they think/feel/believe/know/etc. is right. Therefore, they are able to attribute their personal judgment as religious mandates even when, technically, they are not. Keep in mind that most religions view not doing what you can to survive as a form of despair, which is held to be a much greater sin. Thus, if it comes down to either starving or eating non-kosher/halal food, for example, the religion expressly commands that you eat the food in order to survive. To put this more generally: if you are trapped (mentally or physically) and all your options are sinful, you are obliged to go with the least heinous sin/option. In fantasy and sci-fi settings, religious mandates of various kinds may lead to Fantastic Religious Weirdness , depending on just what those mandates and restrictions are. In case of extrovert Serious Business , see instead Windmill Crusader , Soul-Saving Crusader , Heteronormative Crusader , Moral Guardians , and/or Activist-Fundamentalist Antics . See also Culture Justifies Anything .
